task_yaml.task:
Our university research lab needs a website and I want to pick a Hugo theme
for it. Requirements: it must be bilingual (English + Chinese), it needs a
Team / People page where each lab member has a profile, and it needs a
Publications list for our papers. We'll host it free on GitHub Pages /
Netlify, so it has to be a static Hugo theme with a permissive open-source
license.

A fixed set of candidate themes is provided offline at:

    /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/theme_candidates/

`candidates.json` lists each theme with normalized feature flags, its source,
and an evidence note; the real upstream `theme.toml` manifests are under
`raw_theme_manifests/` so you can verify the flags yourself rather than
trusting the summary. Please read the manifests/evidence — don't pick by
name or popularity.

Work out which themes actually satisfy ALL of the hard requirements
(bilingual i18n, a team/members system, a publications system, permissive
license + static hosting), and pick exactly ONE theme to recommend. Note
that several candidates are tempting for the wrong reason: one has
"Academic" in its name, another is extremely popular — make sure your pick
truly covers the team-members AND publications needs, not just one of them.

Save:
- `/tmp_workspace/results/theme_choice.json`: your recommended `selected_id`,
  why it satisfies every hard requirement (cite the evidence), and the
  minimal steps to get a bilingual lab site with members + publications
  running on it.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/theme_evaluation.json`: per-candidate feature
  coverage against the four hard requirements, with keep/reject and, for each
  rejected theme, exactly which hard requirement it fails.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/theme_method.json`: which manifests/files you read
  and how you verified each feature flag (reproducibility).

This is fully offline — do not fetch anything from the network.
